Package Management — lock files, versioning, dependency auditing
bytes.title: Deno Lock Files & Dependency Auditing — What You Actually Need to Know
bytes.body:
Deno auto-generates `deno.lock` on first run and validates it on every subsequent execution. Unlike npm's lockfile, Deno's lock file stores cryptographic hashes of every remote module — tamper with the source and the runtime refuses to execute. Lock file checks are on by default; opt out with `--no-lock` or set `"lock": false` in `deno.json`.

Versioning lives in `deno.json` under `imports` (the import map). Pin exact versions — `"oak": "jsr:@oak/oak@13.2.5"` — rather than ranges. Deno has no `node_modules` to audit, but `deno info <specifier>` resolves the full dependency graph instantly, printing every transitive dep with its resolved URL.

For security auditing, `deno check` runs type checking across the graph, and `--allow-*` flags enforce least-privilege at runtime. Check your lock file into git; a changed hash on CI is your canary for upstream supply-chain tampering. Run `deno cache --reload` to refresh and regenerate the lock file after intentional upgrades.

bytes.topic: Concurrency Model — async/await, goroutines, threads, actors — how each language handles it
bytes.title: Deno's Concurrency Model — Event Loop, Workers, and Why You Rarely Need Threads
bytes.body:
Deno runs JavaScript on a single-threaded V8 event loop backed by Tokio, Rust's async runtime. Every I/O operation — file reads, HTTP requests, DB queries — is non-blocking by default. You write async/await; Tokio handles the actual OS-level async I/O under the hood.

For CPU-bound parallelism, Deno exposes Web Workers with `new Worker(import.meta.resolve('./worker.ts'), { type: 'module' })`. Each worker gets its own V8 isolate and event loop — true OS threads, no shared heap. Communication is via `postMessage`, and you can pass `SharedArrayBuffer` with `Atomics` for lock-based coordination if needed.

Deno has no goroutines or actor model. Concurrency within a single isolate is purely cooperative — a long synchronous loop blocks everything. The pattern to internalize: keep the main thread for I/O orchestration, offload CPU work to workers. `Promise.all()` for fan-out I/O, workers for fan-out compute. `BroadcastChannel` lets multiple workers subscribe to the same named channel without wiring up individual message ports.

bytes.topic: Type Safety — static vs dynamic typing, type inference, generics across languages
bytes.title: Deno KV — Generics and Type Inference Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body:
Deno ships TypeScript as a first-class runtime — no tsconfig, no ts-node, no build step. That means type safety is structural, not bolted on. Deno's built-in APIs are fully generic: `Deno.Kv.get<T>()` returns `Deno.KvEntryMaybe<T>`, so the inferred type flows through your entire read path without a cast.

Where most runtimes force you to `JSON.parse()` and assert types manually, Deno KV lets you bind a concrete type at the call site. The generic propagates through `.value`, giving you `T | null` rather than `unknown`. Pair this with a thin wrapper and you get a typed data layer with zero schemas or validators at the persistence boundary.

`deno check` runs the TypeScript compiler in isolation — no execution — so you can gate CI on type correctness before any code runs. Static analysis catches `user.email` access on a potentially-null value at compile time, not at runtime in production. Generics across Deno's `fetch`, `KV`, and `serve` APIs follow the same pattern: type once at the boundary, infer everywhere downstream.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — exceptions vs Result types, error propagation patterns per language
bytes.title: Deno Error Handling — Bridging Thrown Errors and Result Types
bytes.body:
Deno's built-in APIs throw typed errors — `Deno.errors.NotFound`, `Deno.errors.PermissionDenied`, `Deno.errors.ConnectionRefused` — unlike Node's callback-style errno codes. This gives you precise catch blocks, but exceptions still break control flow silently.

The Result pattern (a discriminated union `{ ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: E }`) makes error paths explicit in the type signature. The key Deno idiom: wrap throwing stdlib calls in a thin Result factory — catch known `Deno.errors.*` subtypes and convert them to `{ ok: false }`, then re-throw anything unexpected so permission errors still surface loudly.

Propagation works best with early returns: check `if (!result.ok) return result` up the call stack instead of nesting try/catch. For Deno CLI tools, convert back to exceptions at the entry point with `Deno.exit(1)` after logging. For HTTP handlers (`Deno.serve`, Hono, Oak), map the error discriminant to a status code before returning a `Response`.

Avoid catch-all swallowing — `Deno.errors.BadResource` and `Deno.errors.Interrupted` signal very different failure modes and deserve distinct handling.

bytes.topic: Performance — benchmarking, profiling, runtime characteristics per language
bytes.title: Deno Benchmarking with Deno.bench() — Measuring What Actually Matters
bytes.body:
Deno ships a built-in benchmarking harness via `Deno.bench()`, eliminating the need for external tools like benchmark.js. Unlike Node's `perf_hooks`, Deno's bench runner warms up functions automatically, runs multiple iterations, and reports median/min/max with statistical variance — all via `deno bench`.

Each benchmark runs in its own V8 isolate context by default, keeping results clean. Use the `{ warmup: N }` option to control warm-up iterations when testing JIT-sensitive code paths like tight loops or TypedArray operations. For async workloads, return a Promise — Deno handles the await internally.

To profile CPU hotspots, run `deno run --v8-flags=--prof yourscript.ts`, which emits a V8 log processable with `node --prof-process` or `deno_tools`. For memory pressure, `--inspect` + Chrome DevTools heap snapshots work directly against Deno's V8 instance.

Deno's single-threaded event loop means I/O-bound benchmarks behave differently than CPU-bound ones — always isolate them. Use `--allow-hrtime` for high-resolution timing; without it, `performance.now()` is jittered for security.

bytes.topic: Standard Library — what's built in vs what needs a third-party library
bytes.title: Deno's Standard Library — What's Built In vs What You Still Need npm For
bytes.body:
Deno ships with a rich standard library at `jsr:@std/` that covers most day-to-day needs without touching npm. For file I/O, use `Deno.readTextFile` / `Deno.writeTextFile` — no `fs` import needed. HTTP servers? `Deno.serve()` is stable and built-in since Deno 1.35. Need parsing? `@std/csv`, `@std/toml`, `@std/yaml`, and `@std/jsonc` are all available. Cryptography is covered by the Web Crypto API (`crypto.subtle`) plus `@std/crypto` for hashing utilities like `encodeHex`. Date formatting, UUIDs (`@std/uuid`), path manipulation (`@std/path`), and assertion utilities for testing (`@std/assert`) round out the core.

Where the stdlib still falls short: database drivers (reach for `npm:postgres` or `npm:drizzle-orm`), JWT handling (`npm:jose`), email sending, and any cloud SDK. Deno's `npm:` specifier makes these drop-in, but they bypass Deno's permission model — always audit what you're pulling in. The rule of thumb: prefer `jsr:@std/` first, then `jsr:` ecosystem packages, npm as a last resort.

bytes.topic: Runtime & Execution — compiled vs interpreted vs JIT, startup time, cold start
bytes.title: Deno's V8 JIT and What It Means for Cold Starts
bytes.body:
Deno runs TypeScript and JavaScript on V8 — the same engine powering Chrome and Node.js. V8 uses a tiered JIT pipeline: Ignition (bytecode interpreter) handles initial execution, Sparkplug adds a fast baseline compiler, and Maglev/TurboFan kick in for hot code paths. Deno itself is compiled to a native binary (via Rust), so the runtime binary starts instantly — what you're paying for is V8 initialization and your script's parse/compile phase.

For cold starts, the critical lever is `deno compile`. It bundles your app into a standalone executable with a V8 snapshot pre-seeded with your module graph — skipping parse and initial compilation on every invocation. A typical Deno HTTP server drops from ~120ms cold start to ~15ms after compilation.

For workers and edge deployments, Deno Deploy uses isolate pre-warming backed by the same snapshot mechanism. If you're running `deno run` in a hot loop (CI scripts, serverless), prefer `deno compile` or warm cached module graphs via `DENO_DIR` to amortize JIT overhead across runs.

bytes.topic: Memory Management — GC vs ownership model, stack vs heap, memory safety
bytes.title: Deno Memory Model — V8 GC, Rust Ownership, and the FFI Boundary
bytes.body:
Deno's memory model spans two worlds. TypeScript code runs on V8's generational GC — allocations land in the young-generation heap, survivors promote to old-generation, and collection is automatic. Control GC pressure by preferring `ArrayBuffer` transfers (zero-copy ownership transfer) over structured clones when passing data between `Worker`s.

Beneath V8, Deno's runtime is Rust — ownership model, no GC, deterministic drops. This boundary becomes critical at the FFI layer via `Deno.dlopen`. Memory allocated by a native library lives on the C/Rust heap; V8 has zero visibility into it. `Deno.UnsafePointer` gives you a raw handle, but you own the lifetime. Skip your library's `free` equivalent and the leak is silent — no finalizer will save you.

`Deno.memoryUsage()` exposes `heapUsed`, `heapTotal`, `external` (V8-tracked native allocations), and `rss`. When `external` climbs unexpectedly, suspect un-freed FFI allocations. For hot paths, avoid allocating inside tight loops — V8's incremental GC still introduces pauses. Cap heap size in constrained environments with `--v8-flags=--max-old-space-size=512`.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit testing philosophy and test runner per language
bytes.title: Deno's Built-in Test Runner — No Jest, No Config, Just `deno test`
bytes.body:
Deno ships a first-class test runner with zero configuration. You define tests using `Deno.test()`, which accepts a name and an async function. Assertions come from the standard library — `@std/assert` — giving you `assertEquals`, `assertThrows`, `assertRejects`, and more without any third-party install.

Tests run in isolation by default. Each `Deno.test()` call gets its own sanitizer checks: Deno will fail a test if it leaks async ops or spawns unresolved promises — catching bugs that Jest silently misses.

Use `deno test --watch` during development for instant re-runs on file changes. Scope runs with `deno test path/to/file_test.ts` or filter by name with `--filter`. For grouped behavior, nest tests using `t.step()` inside a parent `Deno.test` — Deno's subtests give you hierarchical output without a `describe` wrapper.

Permissions apply to tests too: `deno test --allow-net` is required if your unit under test touches the network, keeping your test surface explicit and auditable.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — linting, formatting, language-specific idioms
bytes.title: Deno's Built-In Linter and Formatter — Zero Config, Maximum Consistency
bytes.body:
Deno ships `deno lint` and `deno fmt` as first-class CLI tools — no ESLint config, no Prettier setup, no `node_modules`. Both run on the same V8-backed runtime, making them fast and predictable across machines.

`deno lint` enforces a curated rule set (based on `eslint` and `typescript-eslint`) with Deno-specific rules like `no-window-prefix` (prefer `globalThis` over `window`) and `ban-untagged-todo`. Run `deno lint --rules` to inspect every active rule.

`deno fmt` uses `dprint` under the hood and is opinionated by design — no semicolons debate, no tab-vs-space wars. Configure deviations only when necessary via `deno.json`:

```json
{ "fmt": { "useTabs": false, "lineWidth": 100, "indentWidth": 2 } }
```

For CI, combine both with `--check` to fail fast without mutating files. Pair with `deno task lint` and `deno task fmt` entries in `deno.json` to give teammates a single entry point. Avoid reaching for external tooling — Deno's built-ins are already version-locked to the runtime.

bytes.topic: Security — memory safety, injection prevention, dependency vulnerability scanning
bytes.title: Deno Security — Permissions, Injection Prevention, and Supply Chain Integrity
bytes.body:
Deno's security model starts at process launch: every dangerous capability requires an explicit `--allow-*` flag. But the runtime API goes further — `Deno.permissions.query()` lets you gate sensitive code paths at runtime, and `Deno.permissions.revoke()` enforces least-privilege by dropping rights after a one-time use.

For injection prevention, never interpolate user input into shell strings. `Deno.Command` takes an `args` array that bypasses the shell entirely — no escaping required, no injection surface. This is the idiomatic Deno replacement for `child_process.exec`.

For dependency integrity, Deno's lockfile (`deno.lock`) records cryptographic hashes of every remote module. Run with `--frozen` in CI to reject any hash mismatch — this catches both accidental drift and supply chain tampering. Combine with an import map to pin specifiers to audited versions, eliminating floating URLs. Unlike `npm audit`, Deno doesn't yet have a built-in CVE scanner, but `deno outdated` surfaces stale deps, and tools like `deno_audit` can integrate with OSV for vulnerability lookups.

bytes.topic: Language-specific — Node.js event loop, Python GIL, Go channels, Rust ownership, Elixir OTP
bytes.title: Deno's Event Loop — How Tokio Powers Your Async Code
bytes.body:
Deno's event loop is backed by Tokio, Rust's async runtime — not libuv like Node.js. This means async operations are scheduled on Tokio's multi-threaded executor, but your JavaScript still runs single-threaded on V8. When you `await` a Deno.readFile() or fetch(), control yields to Tokio, which drives I/O completion via epoll/kqueue, then wakes the V8 isolate to resume your promise chain.

The practical difference: Deno Web Workers each get their own V8 isolate *and* their own Tokio runtime slice — true parallelism without shared memory. Communication uses structured-clone via postMessage(), not shared state. For CPU-bound work, spin a Worker; for I/O concurrency, just fire multiple awaits concurrently with Promise.all().

Op registration is the bridge: every Deno built-in (network, FS, subprocess) is a Rust "op" that crosses the JS/Rust boundary zero-copy where possible, using ArrayBuffer transfer. This is why Deno's I/O benchmarks consistently beat Node on raw throughput — the op layer has lower overhead than libuv's callback dispatch.

bytes.topic: Deployment — binary size, containerization, runtime dependencies
bytes.title: Deno compile — Ship a Single Binary with Zero Runtime Dependencies
bytes.body:
`deno compile` bundles your script, all remote imports, and the Deno runtime into a single executable — no npm install, no node_modules, no Deno on the host. The output is ~80–100 MB (V8 is not small), but that's the only artifact you ship.

For containers, skip the official `denoland/deno` image entirely. Cross-compile to `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` locally, then copy the binary into `gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12` — a ~20 MB base with just glibc. Final image lands around 120 MB versus the ~200 MB `denoland/deno` + source approach.

Permissions are baked in at compile time via `--allow-net`, `--allow-env`, etc. — no flags needed at runtime. For ARM deploys (e.g., AWS Graviton), target `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu` from your local machine. Use `--no-terminal` on Windows targets to suppress the console window.

One hard limit: `deno compile` does not support `npm:` specifiers that rely on native Node addons — those still require the full Deno runtime on the host.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Cross-compile from Mac/Windows for Linux
deno compile \
  --target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu \
  --allow-net --allow-env \
  --output server \
  main.ts

# Dockerfile — zero Deno runtime needed at deploy
FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12
COPY server /server
EXPOSE 8000
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — REPL, debugger, language server protocol support
bytes.title: Deno's Built-in Dev Toolchain: REPL, Inspector, and LSP With Zero Config
bytes.body:
Deno ships a complete dev toolchain as first-class CLI commands — no separate installs, no plugin ecosystem to manage.

`deno repl` drops you into an interactive session. Pass `--eval` to pre-import modules before the prompt opens, and supply permission flags just like a normal script. JSR imports resolve on the fly: `deno repl --allow-net --eval 'import { encodeBase64 } from "jsr:@std/encoding/base64"'` gives you `encodeBase64` immediately at the `>` prompt.

For debugging, `deno run --inspect-brk main.ts` pauses execution at the first line and exposes a WebSocket endpoint. Open `chrome://inspect` and select the Deno target — full Chrome DevTools: breakpoints, call stack, heap snapshots. No launch.json required for basic use.

The LSP (`deno lsp`) is what the official VS Code extension and `nvim-lspconfig`'s `denols` preset connect to. It reads your `deno.json` for compiler options, import map, lint rules, and fmt config, giving you accurate type-checked completions across JSR, npm specifiers, and local modules — without a `node_modules` directory in sight.

bytes.topic: Scalability — horizontal scaling characteristics, thread model implications
bytes.title: Deno's Single-Isolate Thread Model and What It Means for Horizontal Scaling
bytes.body:
Each Deno process runs a single V8 isolate with one event loop thread — there is no thread pool backing `Deno.serve()` the way Node.js cluster mode works. I/O concurrency is high because the event loop never blocks, but CPU-bound work saturates that one thread immediately.

True parallelism requires explicit `Worker` instances. Each `new Worker(...)` spawns a fresh V8 isolate with its own event loop. Workers share no heap by default; you pass data via `postMessage` (structured clone) or hand off a `SharedArrayBuffer` for zero-copy coordination. `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` gives you the logical CPU count to size your pool.

For horizontal scaling across machines, Deno's stateless-by-default model is an asset: each process holds no implicit shared state, so adding replicas behind a load balancer is friction-free. Deno Deploy leans into this fully — each request can land on a fresh isolate in a different region. The implication: externalize all mutable state (Postgres, Redis, KV) from the start. Sticky sessions or in-process caches will break under replicas.

bytes.topic: Ecosystem Maturity — library quality, community size, long-term viability
bytes.title: Deno's Ecosystem in 2025 — JSR, npm Compat, and Long-Term Bets
bytes.body:
Deno's ecosystem maturity story changed sharply with Deno 2.0 and the JSR registry. The legacy deno.land/x registry is frozen — all new packages land on jsr.io, where provenance, scoring, and TypeScript-first publishing are built in. The standard library (`jsr:@std/`) hit 1.0 across core modules (path, fs, http, assert, streams), meaning breakage risk is now low for day-to-day use.

The npm compatibility layer (`npm:` specifiers) is production-grade. You can drop in Express, Prisma, or sharp without a shim — Deno resolves them from npm's registry, applies its permission model, and types flow through automatically. This eliminates the historic "the package doesn't exist for Deno" blocker.

Community size remains smaller than Node's, but the signal-to-noise ratio is higher: Deno Deploy usage is growing, Fresh 2 ships with built-in islands, and Deno is now the runtime behind several Supabase Edge Functions. The company backing, V8 engine stability, and W3C API alignment make long-term viability credible — you're not betting on a runtime that could vanish.

bytes.topic: When to Choose — use cases where each language excels vs where it struggles
bytes.title: Deno — When It Shines and When to Think Twice
bytes.body:
Deno excels at secure-by-default server workloads where you want zero npm install friction. Its permission model (`--allow-net`, `--allow-read`) makes it ideal for CLI tools, webhook handlers, and internal APIs where least-privilege matters. Native TypeScript without `tsc` config, a built-in formatter, linter, and test runner (`deno test`) collapse the toolchain to a single binary — perfect for small teams or solo services. Deno Deploy pairs naturally for globally distributed edge functions with sub-10ms cold starts.

Where it struggles: large enterprise monorepos that depend heavily on CommonJS packages — `npm:` specifiers help, but compatibility gaps still surface. Node.js-native addons (`.node` files) don't run. If your team has deep Express/Fastify muscle memory, Deno's `Deno.serve()` API and `jsr:` registry require a mental context switch. For CPU-bound tasks needing native threading, Node worker_threads with native modules still outperforms. Choose Deno when security posture, first-class TypeScript, and lean tooling outweigh ecosystem breadth.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — runtime metrics, profiling in production, GC pauses
bytes.title: Deno Runtime Metrics — Profiling Memory, Ops, and GC in Production
bytes.body:
Deno exposes two built-in primitives most developers overlook: `Deno.memoryUsage()` and `Deno.metrics()`. The former returns `rss`, `heapTotal`, `heapUsed`, and `external` — direct V8 heap stats, no third-party agent needed. The latter yields per-op counters (`opsDispatched`, `opsCompleted`, `bytesSentControl`, `bytesReceivedControl`), letting you spot async op backlogs that signal I/O starvation before they hit latency SLOs.

GC pauses are invisible by default. Pass `--v8-flags=--trace-gc` at startup to stream GC events to stderr — pipe them to a structured log aggregator (e.g. Loki) and alert on any scavenge exceeding 10ms. For deeper heap snapshots in production, launch with `--inspect=0.0.0.0:9229` behind a firewall and connect Chrome DevTools remotely; `Runtime.takeHeapSnapshot` works over the CDP without restarting the process.

For continuous monitoring, emit `Deno.memoryUsage()` on a fixed interval and push the payload to your metrics backend via a simple HTTP POST — no SDK, no daemon, no sidecar.
